data.title: Rose's Income Garden November Raise: Verizon And Still A Buy
data.description:
November Income: 23 Payers

Rose’s Income Garden “RIG” portfolio has 78 investments and carries a 6.4% forward dividend income yield. Raises are very important to that income, and the higher, the better.

12 quarterly payments mark the end of the February, May, August, and November ('FMAN') sequence, with Verizon being the only raise received. The remainder were the 11 monthly high-yield payers discussed here in a recent article to get to 23 total for the month.

The most popular quarterly payment sequence is for the months of March, June, September, and December, which I call MJSD, with RIG having a total of 28 plus the monthlys to delightfully end December 2025. Therefore, when I do comparisons, I will only look from quarter to quarter. November was a low-paying month but was still slightly higher than its comparative payment month of August. The RIG current yield is 5.8%, and I will mention once again that it has a forward yield of 6.4%.

Review of Verizon and some competition

The chart below shows 4 communication sector companies that I actually owned this last year, showing each current dividend statistic and yield. I will reveal later the 2 sold, giving you a chance to guess which ones I sold.

Curr $Price = the stock price on December 3, 2025.

Div $ share = the most recent dividend paid.

Yrly $ Div = the current yearly dividend total.

Div% = Dividend yield using statistics in the chart

Curr

1 RAISE

Verizon Communications, Inc.

The company was formerly known as Bell Atlantic Corporation and changed its name to Verizon in June 2000. It was incorporated in 1983 and is headquartered in New York, New York. It has an S&P credit rating of BBB+. It provides wireless services and technology to businesses and private customers worldwide.

This was its 21st consecutive raise which was 1.25c from 67.75c to 69c which = 1.84%. It has a 5 year dividend growth rate of 1.98% as taken from FAST Graphs, a service to which I subscribe.

The raise at the current price gives it a yield of 6.8% and is also noted in the chart above.

The Chowder # which measures an investment purchase status by adding the 5 year dividend growth rate and yield is in this case 1.98 + 1.84 to = 8.78. An 8 is the suggested minimum # desired which it easily passes.

Seeking Alpha 1 year technical and price chart

Next I will show a 4 year FAST Graphs price and technical chart.

The black line is the price.

The dark green area represents earnings.

The blue line is the normal P/E line

The yellow line represents the dividend and shows it is well covered by earnings.

Dotted lines and any E behind a number represent estimates.

The FAST Graph shows the current P/E to be 8.67x and the normal 8.36x.

It would seem, using the P/E, the price is just a bit above normal.

Rose Recommendation

Most of the metrics presented continue to point to Verizon being a quality, reliable, and nice high yielding company. It is very buyable if you want a great income stock and don’t own it. I do own it and enjoy it very much, but watch for a 7% yield to add on for me once again.

3 Competitors

1- BCE Inc.

This telecommunications sector company provides wireless, wire line, internet, streaming services, and television (TV) services to residential, business, and wholesale customers in Canada. It was founded in 1880 and is headquartered in Verdun, QC.

The very sad news from BCE is that it cut its dividend this year down to 32c from ~69c. The price has compensated for that cut to lower, which now gives it a nice yield of ~5.4%. I have held on and will continue to do so but will watch it closely. I do believe cutting the dividend was the right management move. It does have an ADR fee, and dividends may vary by the foreign exchange rate.

2- ATT Inc.

This company is well known and has been around with different names for years. The company was formerly known as SBC Communications Inc. and changed its name to AT&T Inc. in 2005. AT&T Inc. was incorporated in 1983 and is based in Dallas, Texas, and has an S&P credit rating of BBB.

If you guessed I sold this one, you were right. It has not raised the dividend for many years now, and it probably will remain as such for a few more. This was an excellent income stock, but now, sadly, it does not provide the yield of almost 6-7% it had so many years ago. I sold T twice this past year at $28 and $29.06 and don’t regret it, as it has done just what I thought it would do, and the yield remains low.

3- Vodafone Group Public Limited Company

Lastly, this is the other one I sold. Vodafone offers services in Germany, the United Kingdom, much of Europe, Turkey, and South Africa. It was incorporated in 1984 and is based in Newbury, the United Kingdom, and has an S&P credit rating of BBB. It pays dividends twice yearly and most recently cut them. The next installment is 23.9c to be paid February 5, 2026. (Don’t hurry to get it if you want it, as the ex-date was Nov. 24, 2025). It also has an ADR fee.

Below is the 5 year SA chart to show its decline over the past years.

I sold this in August 2025 for $11.38 and had a yield then of 4.2%. The price is slowly rising up a bit, making the yield actually lower now and under 4%. The SA chart is not correct with the dividend rate, so the yield is correct in my chart above for 3.88%. I enjoyed ~6% before the cut, but management did do the right thing by cutting the dividend. I never enjoyed the twice a year dividends and strange ex-dates, so I have no regrets about selling it other than it took me too long to do it.

Summary/Conclusion

Having dividend cuts in a portfolio is not advantageous, and I certainly hope I have learned the lesson. I will continue to look for companies with quality cash flows into the future. I did learn S&P credit ratings may be kept if a company cuts the dividend to maintain a lower payout, and it alone is not exactly a sure investment metric to rely on. RIG continues to do well and is up 13.3% YTD, and as mentioned, income has been quite reliable at 5.8% this year.

data.title: A Major Market Disconnect Is Forming
data.description:
While the S&P 500 (SPY) is trading near all-time highs, key underlying economic data seems to be indicating that a major market disconnect is forming. In this article, I will look at this disconnect and share how I am allocating capital in light of the disconnect.

U.S. Economic Data Points To Stagflation

The U.S. economy appears to be entering a stagflationary slowdown, as evidenced by weakening production and persistent inflation. The latest evidence of this came from the ISM Manufacturing PMI in November, which fell to 48.2 from 48.7 in October, marking the ninth straight month of manufacturing sector contraction. A particularly weak reading came in from New Orders, which declined from 48.7 to 47.4 month-over-month, which indicates weakening future demand. On top of that, the Employment Index dropped to a three-month low of 44, down from 46 sequentially, which also shows that declines in headcount are accelerating, likely due to uncertainty over near-to-mid-term demand.

Despite this weakening of industrial activity, inflation remains elevated, with the Prices Index at 58.5 in November, up from 58 in October, which shows that even as demand is weakening, manufacturers are having to pay more for production inputs, which points directly to a stagflationary environment emerging, especially due to headwinds from tariffs and uncertainties over trade policies. Retailers are also seeing reduced demand, which helps to explain why new orders are weakening so much, and businesses are citing increased costs for insurance, utilities, and healthcare, on top of rising input costs for manufacturers.

A lone bright spot was seen in the S&P Global U.S. Manufacturing PMI, which came in at 52.2, but this was still down from 52.5 in October, and S&P Global’s Chief Business Economist stated,

Although the headline PMI signalled a further expansion of factory activity in November, the health of the US manufacturing sector gets more worrying the more you scratch under the surface.

In particular, the slowdown in New Orders reflects a buildup in finished goods inventories. A point of weakness moving forward is that the output increase is likely being driven more by the clearing of backlogs rather than true new demand.

The Cause: Malinvestment and Oversupply

There are likely several causes for this, but one of the main ones is likely due to years of low-interest rates from the Fed’s policy. Easy money policies have driven malinvestment and overconsumption by individuals and businesses, and now, with interest rates at more normalized levels for an extended period of time, consumption is pulling back, thereby driving demand lower and hurting companies in the process.

Why the Market Disconnect Spells Danger

So what does this mean for the market? Well, it certainly is not positive, as a stagflation environment means that long-term interest rates are likely to remain higher, which puts pressure on equity valuations, while economic weakness means that growth for businesses is likely to take a hit.

This is especially troublesome given that it comes at a time when, by virtually every major market valuation metric, the S&P 500 is materially overvalued. This means that the market may be due for a major pullback or, at the very least, years of very weak performance. In fact, Howard Marks recently cautioned that the next decade could see annualized real returns of near zero, based on historical precedent whenever markets have reached their current valuation levels.

Aligning Capital for Stagflation Resistance and AI Growth

So does this mean that the markets are doomed? In my view, there are two possible outcomes for the economy and market. One, Elon Musk recently predicted that there is considerable potential for the economy to experience a deflationary boom. If AI can come to the rescue and, in a relatively short number of years, deliver massive productivity gains that fight off inflation and drive robust growth even as prices fall, the market may do fine moving forward.

However, absent some major leaps in agentic AI and robotics capabilities, I think we are much more likely headed for stagflation, and I expect stagflation to hit the economy at least in the near term. Therefore, while I am placing some bets on the AI boom, particularly in the infrastructure (UTF) space, I am also aligning those bets as closely as possible with business models that are likely to weather a stagflationary environment just fine. In particular, I like infrastructure businesses (UTG) that have long-duration cash flows with inflation-linked escalators, as the long-duration cash flows and investment-grade balance sheets should hold up well during periods of economic weakness, while the inflation-linked components of the business should enable cash flows to grow at a rate that keeps up with inflation. Meanwhile, these infrastructure businesses, by being linked to the AI boom, will also still likely do just fine in a deflationary environment in which AI fuels the growth and deflation. This is because these businesses should be benefiting from significant demand for their services to underpin the AI-dominated economy.

In particular, I like stocks like Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP)(BEPC), as the vast majority of its cash flow is contracted and indexed to inflation, and it has a BBB+ credit rating with strong diversification across numerous power production segments that should benefit significantly from the AI boom, as they already have agreements with Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) and Microsoft (MSFT) in place and expect to have additional power provision agreements with other hyperscalers moving forward. I also like midstream companies (AMLP) that have material natural gas exposure, like ONEOK (OKE) as they expect to win several natural gas contracts with data center projects to fuel the AI boom. OKE also has a highly contracted cash flow profile, investment-grade balance sheet, and inflation-linked escalators in some of their pipeline tariffs that should help them keep up with inflation as well should we enter a sustained stagflationary environment.

Investor Takeaway

While the market seems to be betting heavily on an AI boom, the underlying economic data reveal that there appears to be a major market disconnect as the manufacturing sector continues to contract and the jobs market remains quite weak. Therefore, unless AI can deliver on its promise in a relatively short period of time, it appears we are likely headed for a period of stagflation. This sort of environment will likely lead to a material pullback or at least a prolonged period of underperformance by the S&P 500.

As a result, we are steering clear of the relatively poor risk-reward profile offered by major indexes and instead are loading up on stagflation-resistant yet AI-adjacent infrastructure businesses in order to continue generating attractive income and total returns in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Carrefour: A Defensive Income Play For 2026
data.description:
Introduction

With markets trading close to all-time highs, defensive stocks such as Carrefour SA (OTCPK:CRRFY, CA.PA) have naturally not been on the radar for investors. This is actually quite good if you want to add downside protection since defensive stocks trade at attractive valuations relative to the overall market.

As discussed in my previous coverage on Carrefour, the company's dividend remains a key appeal for investors. Looking ahead to 2026, I remain bullish on Carrefour, with my Buy rating driven by:

  • 2026 free cash benefitting from a one-off refinancing of debt in Brazil, implying that the trailing payout ratio stands at just 40%.
  • Coupled with a low single-digit underlying growth rate of sales and earnings, this should result in a high single-digit/low double-digit increase in dividends.
  • Increased focus on current income and earnings sustainability amid an expected moderation in broad stock market returns over the medium term.

In this article, I will review the company's Q3 2025 sales update, discuss the outlook for 2026 free cash flow, and provide an update on Carrefour's valuation. Last but not least, I highlight key risks in the investment case.

Company Overview

You can access all Carrefour results on its website here. Carrefour operates over 14,300 stores around the world, primarily in the form of convenience stores, supermarkets, and hypermarkets:

France remains the single largest market, accounting for 52% of Q3 2025 sales. Other European countries contribute 24% of sales, with the remaining 24% of sales coming from Latin America, mainly Brazil:

The company's net debt position varies significantly during the year due to changes in working capital, as well as the dividend payment in H1 2025. Even so, assuming Carrefour hits its free cash flow target for 2025 (i.e., free cash flow of around €1.5 billion), I estimate Carrefour should end 2025 with a net debt of around €3.4 billion, indicating a total enterprise value of €12.9 billion when we factor in the €9.5 billion market capitalization.

As such, I anticipate leverage at Carrefour to remain modest, accounting for around 26% of enterprise value at year-end 2025.

Q3 2025 Results Overview

Carrefour reported a 1.5% Y/Y decline in Q3 2025 sales, impacted primarily by strength in the Euro relative to Latin America currencies (-2.8% FX impact), as well as a 0.6% decline due to business disposals, partially offset by a 2.1% increase in like-for-like sales:

Given structurally high interest rates and inflation in Brazil and Argentina, foreign exchange will likely remain a drag on reported results over the medium to long term.

Looking at performance across regions, France and other European countries reported a like-for-like ex petrol and calendar effects growth of 0.7% and 1% respectively, which is somewhat underwhelming as it is below the rate of inflation. The silver lining is that inflation in France has been quite muted recently, at 0.9% in October 2025.

Growth in Latin America remained robust at 5.5%, although this was negated by currency effects, as discussed above:

Bullish 2025-2026 Outlook

Against this backdrop, the company confirmed its 2025 financial targets, envisioning a slight increase in EBITDA and net free cash flow (2024: €1.457 billion). This will be partially driven by debt refinancing in Brazil, as discussed in the results press release:

In Brazil, the Group has already refinanced €1.4bn of its Brazilian real-denominated debt, representing almost all of Carrefour Brazil's c.€1.5bn external debt. The repurchase of the remaining €100m is underway. This transaction is expected to generate an annual positive impact of around €100m on net free cash-flow and on the Group's net income from 2026 onwards, with an impact of €20m to €25m expected as early as 2025

As such, I see net free cash flow around €1.6 billion in 2026. The net result will ultimately depend on how proceeds from exiting the Italian market (expected to close in Q4 2025) are set to be used.

Carrefour Valuation

Taking into account my €1.6 billion free cash flow estimate for 2026, we observe that Carrefour offers a free cash flow yield of around 16.8% relative to the current market capitalization of €9.5 billion. As discussed in the Company Overview section, this is achieved with modest leverage.

From a growth perspective, Carrefour's free cash flow expansion will depend primarily on efficiency gains, as well as progress from its strategic review (as evidenced by Italy disposals). In any case, outside of the one-off refinancing effect in Brazil, I think it will be hard for Carrefour to grow free cash flow faster than a low single-digit rate (on an enterprise-level basis).

On a per share basis, cash flow growth is likely to be stronger as the 2025 dividend represents a payout of just 40% relative to my free cash flow estimate for 2026 of €1.6 billion. Taking into account significant retained earnings, I anticipate robust dividend growth in the high single digits/low double digits in the years ahead, as was the case over the past decade. Indeed we observe that in USD terms, Carrefour's 6.72% dividend has grown at 11.02% compounded over the past ten years:

As long as the company can maintain its current operating performance, retained earnings and low-single-digit sales growth should support a comparable dividend increase in the future as well.

To conclude, despite weak underlying growth, I think that the market is currently underappreciating the resilience of Carrefour's earnings. This is quite normal given the prevailing bullish sentiment. As we all know, market sentiment changes eventually. Should a focus on current income and earnings sustainability emerge in the future, I believe that Carrefour will be one of the defensive companies on investors' radar.

Also taking into account my positive 2026 free cash flow outlook, I think that the opportunities outweigh the risks going into next year, confirming a Buy rating on Carrefour.

Risks

Food and convenience retail is a highly competitive business. As such, maintaining reasonable margins is key for long-term free cash flow growth. The Q3 2025 results only included a sales update which does not focus on profitability. As such, the latest available figures are from H1 2025, where we observe a small 0.2% improvement in the company's gross margin to 19.6%, also reflected in a 1.1% Y/Y increase in recurring operating income before depreciation/ amortization:

Hence we can conclude that Carrefour is able to achieve marginally stronger profitability despite weak sales growth. This is consistent with my underlying assumption that the company will deliver sales and earnings growth in the low single digits.

From a geographic perspective, we note Carrefour's outsized exposure to France, at 52% of Q3 2025 sales. As such, developments in the French economy, as well as the 2027 Presidential Elections, are key points of attention.

Latin America remains Carrefour's growth engine, although recent euro strength has resulted in negative reported sales growth despite strong underlying increases on a like-for-like local currency basis. Given persistent high interest rates and inflation in Latin America, this is likely to remain the case for the foreseeable future.

Conclusion

Carrefour has delivered an impressive ~21% gain in USD terms so far this year, benefiting from steady operating performance as well as a strong EUR. This is well above the ~2% gain for the State Street Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLP) (which given its focus on U.S. stocks is an imperfect peer):

Looking ahead to 2026, Carrefour's valuation remains very undemanding. Indeed, significant retained earnings, coupled with modest underlying growth, should allow Carrefour to grow its 6.72% dividend in the high single digits/low double digits.

I confirm my Buy rating on Carrefour as I see equity market gains moderating substantially after several years of outsized returns. In this environment, I believe the resilience of Carrefour's earnings will once again capture investors' attention.

Key risks in the investment case include intense competition in Carrefour's markets, resulting in a limited room for margin expansion, as well as exposure to France and Latin America, which bring idiosyncratic political and economic risks.

Thank you for reading.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dell-IP Storage Fuels Growth And Protects Margins -- What Investors Should Know
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my last coverage of Dell Technologies (DELL), I maintained a bullish view based on the company’s robust growth in the Infrastructure Solutions Group ("ISG") and the strategic actions taken to address weaknesses in both the storage business and the Client Solutions Group ("CSG").

I also highlighted the company’s low profit margins and its attractive valuation supported by its overall strong momentum in ISG.

In this article, I will explain why I continue to hold a bullish view. From Oct. 27, 2025, until the end of November, Dell’s stock price has decreased by about 17%, with concerns over a potential future margin reduction becoming the main issue for investors. The storage business line remains a key factor for further margin expansion, while the CSG segment continues to be my primary risk concern.

Higher Memory Prices Pressure Dell’s Margins

First, let me summarize the company’s current margin and profitability status. Dell’s gross margin stood at 20.7% in Q3 FY26, up compared to the previous quarter but down YoY. However, in terms of operating performance, the company reported a 7.8% margin in Q3 FY26, compared to 6% in Q2 FY26 and 7.1% in Q3 FY25, which signals that Dell achieved to reduce OpEx and benefit from the scaling of its operations — lower cost per unit. In absolute terms, Dell reported net income of $1.5 billion, up 32% vs. $1.17 billion in Q3 FY25. Although the company faces increased operating costs, it has managed to sustain and even expand its profitability.

Diving into the segments, I noticed that the main driver behind the company’s gross margin reduction is the significantly higher cost of goods sold (COGS) in the ISG segment. Specifically, COGS reached $13.16 billion in Q3 FY26, up about 62% YoY compared to $8.13 billion in Q3 FY25. The rest of the expenses remained at the same level for both segments.

The main reason for the increased COGS is the higher prices of DRAM and NAND memory, which are crucial hardware components for AI infrastructure workloads. These memories are a core part of the AI hardware stack, and in combination with the massive data center demand and the limited supply across the broader memory industry, they have pushed their prices significantly higher.

Notably, Samsung has raised its memory prices by 60% compared to September 2025, and a report from Counterpoint Research forecasts that DRAM prices will double from Q1’25 to the end of CY26, signaling that Dell will have to deal with elevated COGS for the coming quarters.

To summarize, Dell is facing gross margin pressure due to increased memory prices — primarily DRAM and NAND — which are crucial components in AI infrastructure. However, its profitability and operating margin remain strong thanks to the company’s disciplined expense management.

Servers Drive Growth But Storage (Could) Drive Margins

It is well known that revenue from the ISG segment has already exceeded the more traditional CSG. With a closer look at Q3’25 results, I notice that CSG revenue accounted for $12.1 billion, while ISG revenue was $11.36 billion. In Q3’26, CSG reported about $12.5 billion, while ISG reported $14.1 billion, which means a YoY growth of 3% and 35%, respectively.

The company has strong momentum in the AI infrastructure market, but although revenue growth is robust, its margins remain at relatively low levels, as mentioned above— but Dell continues to expand consistently. In my view, a key reason for this steady margin expansion is the reduction in expenses, mainly in SG&A, along with a hidden factor: Dell-IP storage.

Dell, like all companies, can only reduce its expenses to a limit; therefore, the company has to find other ways to expand its margins in the future. So, Dell has already found them.

Based on the company’s statement:

Our ISG operating income rate was up 360 basis points sequentially to 12.4% of revenue. This improvement was driven by mix of AI servers, sequential improvement in AI server margins and stronger profitability from storage…The mix continues to increase across our storage revenue dollars. The margins within the portfolio continue to improve…So I think PowerMax, PowerScale, PowerStore, ObjectScale and PowerFlex all growing. We've talked about PowerStore in 7 quarters of growth, 6 of those double digits

Let me explain. Dell-IP storage is a set of branded hardware—such as arrays and all-flash storage—and is usually combined with software for management, automation, backup, or cybersecurity purposes.

Using its Dell-IP storage set of hardware and software (or Dell-IP ecosystem) in AI infrastructure projects can benefit the company because the Dell-IP hardware are premium products compared to the market, according to the company. This gives the company space to maintain higher prices for these products, supporting the company’s margins.

The combination of Dell-IP storage hardware and the company’s strong momentum in the AI infrastructure market could drive further margin expansion. I believe Dell is well-positioned to grow its branded storage solutions through a strong ISG segment.

As an investor who already holds a position in DELL, I would like to see whether the company can continue to increase the share of revenue coming from the Dell-IP system. The positive news is that the company expects further growth in Dell-IP, based on the company’s CFO statement:

Look, we expect to continue to make progress. Our Q4 profit guidance is anchored again through the storage P&L. With the Dell-IP storage growth, we expect to make it 4 for 4 in terms of quarterly growth in that portfolio. That should allow us to grow at or likely slightly ahead of normal sequentials, which will allow us to see an uptick in our op inc rate sequentially also into Q4.

Valuation

In my valuation analysis, I consider the company’s momentum. Given that Dell shows strong momentum with a backlog of $18.4 billion, AI shipments of $5.6 billion, and new orders of $12.3 billion in Q3’26, this reflects the company’s high demand for its ISG services. Notably, Dell estimates AI shipments of $25 billion for FY26, a YoY increase of 150%.

The company’s valuation seems even more attractive following the recent dip. Traditional ratios, such as P/E, EV/Sales, and P/S, all indicate that Dell remains significantly undervalued compared to the sector.

In terms of growth, the company is expected to achieve double-digit revenue growth over the next two years. Additionally, Dell anticipates revenue growth of 17% for FY26, slightly above analysts’ estimate of 16.57%. In my view, Dell has the potential to exceed this expectation.

Lastly, while Dell’s gross margin remains below the sector average, its EBIT and net profit margins are higher than the sector, reflecting the company’s ability to control expenses and operate efficiently, despite price pressures from increased DRAM and NAND memory costs.

Overall, the company seems undervalued in my view, considering its growth momentum and the significant potential for margin expansion, mainly through the Dell-IP storage ecosystem. Low margins remain the main risk concern for analysts and investors; however, I believe Dell can manage them effectively. Investors should closely monitor the trend in storage revenue, as well as the company’s overall margin trajectory. That said, margins are not my main risk, but they are not the only one.

Risk Analysis

My main risk is not the low margins, because I believe Dell can increase them through support from the storage hardware and disciplined expense management. Especially considering that the “all-flash portion of the portfolio is growing double digits” for the second consecutive quarter — the flagship product of Dell’s branded storage ecosystem.

My main concern is the company’s traditional segment, the CSG, which includes branded hardware such as PCs, laptops, monitors, and general peripheral devices. The CSG segment remained almost flat in Q3’26, with a revenue growth of 3% YoY, but its operating income has been continuously decreasing over the last quarters. Overall, the segment has not contributed significantly to the company’s profitability.

In my view, Dell has to balance the growth of this segment without negatively impacting its segment margin and, consequently, the company’s overall profit margin. The company noted that the PC market is expected to remain and that it lost a portion of market share in the non-premium segment but increased its share in the commercial premium segment. I believe the company’s focus on premium products is strategic, as premium PCs and laptops have better margins than non-premium ones.

However, I see that the overall CSG segment remains weak and does not contribute — or may even negatively contribute — to the company’s overall profitability. Any change in demand for AI infrastructure could result in a significant slowdown in the ISG revenue segment, and CSG would not be able to support the company’s growth outlook.

Although the possibility of a slowdown in AI infrastructure is limited, given the broad AI market demand and the company’s strong backlog, investors should consider the company’s weak CSG segment.

Currently, there is an opportunity for Dell to boost its revenue from the end of Windows 10 support, with potentially 1 billion PCs as candidates for upgrade or replacement, as well as from AI PCs enhanced by small language models.

Conclusion

In conclusion, Dell remains an undervalued stock with impressive revenue growth, primarily in the ISG segment, while the CSG segment remains weak. Additionally, the company’s Dell-IP ecosystem could further boost margins, and investors should monitor the trajectory of the storage business line.

The risk of a weak CSG segment is offset by opportunities from replacement PCs and laptops due to the end of Windows 10 support, as well as from AI-enhanced PCs.

Overall, considering the company’s risk-reward profile, valuation based on growth and value metrics, and future growth prospects, Dell remains attractive in my view. Therefore, I maintain Dell’s stock rating as "Buy."

data.title: Catalyst: Firdapse Cliff Deferred, But Capital Allocation Still An Issue (Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Today, I am revisiting Catalyst Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (CPRX). I last wrote about this company in September 2024. Things were looking bleak at that time. Catalyst’s flagship drug, FIRDAPSE, appeared to be on the verge of losing market exclusivity amid a legal battle with Teva Pharmaceutical (TEVA).

Its other drugs, AGAMREE and FYCOMPA, faced competition from a few other branded and generic drugs in their respective markets. FYCOMPA, like FIRDAPSE, faced a patent cliff, too.

For these reasons, I sided with caution (Sell rating) and posited that its stock “very much relies on management’s ability to strike great deals.”

Catalyst’s stock has since underperformed the S&P 500 index (SPY) by a score of roughly +19% to +10%.

In the article that follows, I discuss some recent developments that support a more neutral stance.

Recent Developments

A lot has happened in the past year for Catalyst.

First and foremost, in January, Catalyst settled with Teva. Teva received a license to market generic FIRDAPSE no earlier than 2035. In August, Catalyst reached a similar agreement with Lupin, another generic drug manufacturer. Effectively, the FIRDAPSE LOE risk I highlighted last year has been deferred for roughly a decade, barring an adverse outcome in the remaining Hetero litigation (a trial is expected for March 2026).

Why all the drama, though?

Recall that FIRDAPSE was first FDA-approved for adults with LEMS in November 2018. Orphan exclusivity ended in November 2025. However, Catalyst has multiple method-of-use (MOU) and dosing patents (Orange Book) that extend to 2037. However, MOU and dosing patents aren’t as “solid” as composition-of-matter patents on a new molecule. Under Hatch-Waxman, ANDA filers have to either wait until those patents expire or file a Paragraph IV challenge claiming the patents are invalid/not infringed and litigate. That’s what Teva, Lupin, and Hetero have done. Rather than litigate to a “final judgment,” Catalyst and Teva/Lupin have “shaken hands.” Of course, Hetero remains a “wild card,” but the largest competitors are contractually locked out until 2035.

These developments were critical because FIRDAPSE makes up ~60% of Catalyst’s total revenues. FIRDAPSE 2025 revenue guidance was reaffirmed between $355 million and $360 million.

Turning to AGAMREE, its launch is outpacing my expectations. Recall that I argued that AGAMREE was unlikely to approach EMFLAZA’s ~$255 million 2023 sales given limited differentiation and generic prednisone pressure. EMFLAZA, too, has a generic version available. Now, both EMFLAZA and AGAMREE are generally preferred over prednisone due to a better side effect profile. In Q3 2025, AGAMREE net revenue rose 115% Y/Y to $32.4 million. Catalyst is guiding for $105 million to $115 million for the full-year 2025.

While AGAMREE’s strength is real, depth is the next test. During the Q3 call, management stated that all top 45 DMD centers have at least one AGAMREE patient. Most of those patients came from either prednisone or from EFLAZA. So, AGAMREE’s tolerability narrative is really resonating with the DMD community. For now, payers are cooperative, with over 85% reimbursement success. That said, the key “swing factor” over the next few years is whether payers eventually push harder on step therapy versus cheaper steroids.

Meanwhile, FYCOMPA is clearly ex-growth. While there is only one generic so far, with a small discount (Teva’s generic is ~17% below WAC), more are expected to arrive, causing “additional erosion.” Despite this, FYCOMPA 2025 revenue guidance was raised to between $100 million and $110 million.

Strategy

We have to recognize Catalyst for what it is and isn’t. It isn’t a classic growth biotech. As I’ve pointed out in the past, the company doesn’t have its own “drug machine” and allocates very little to R&D (just $2.657 million in Q3 despite $148.392 million in total revenues). Catalyst looks more like a high-margin specialty pharma compounder.

In lieu of internal R&D, Catalyst always has an eye open for deals (in-licensing drugs, acquisitions, co-development, and so on) and new indications.

Regarding the latter, management hinted towards an oncology-LEMS exploration for FIRDAPSE. Earlier this year, the NCCN explicitly called out LEMS as a paraneoplastic syndrome in SCLC and specifically lists FIRDAPSE as the recommended symptomatic treatment.

While this represents a new market for FIRDAPSE, the addressable patient pool is intrinsically small and limited by SCLC incidence and survival (median survival often under 2 years). For instance, it is estimated that ~3% of SCLC (U.S. incidence of ~25-30k/year) have LEMS. So, this is several hundred patients. While oncology-LEMS can support modest growth going forward, I wouldn't expect a major boost from it. Another challenge is that many of these patients go undiagnosed. Although NCCN efforts increase awareness, this remains a commercial hurdle for FIRDAPSE in oncology-LEMS.

Financial Health

Catalyst has reportedly mulled over ~100 business development, or BD, assessments, but hasn’t pulled the trigger on anything lately. Instead, opting for a $200 million buyback through December 31, 2026. As of September 30, 2025, Catalyst had $689.9 million in cash and equivalents with no material long-term debt.

Q3 2025 total revenue came in at $148.4 million, up ~15% Y/Y, driven by FIRDAPSE ($92.2 million, +16% Y/Y), AGAMREE ($32.4 million, +115% Y/Y), and FYCOMPA ($23.8 million, -26% Y/Y). GAAP net income was $52.8 million, up 20% Y/Y.

Valuation Assumptions

Per my DCF model, CPRX appears undervalued on a cash flow basis.

For assumptions, I went with 5% "Revenue Growth," or CAGR (terminating at 2% after the nine-year projection) and a 35% "FCF Margin." This is consistent with CPRX’s recent performance.

"Year 1 Revenue" represents the consensus revenue estimate for 2026. Since we are still one quarter away from 2026, the "Fraction of Year Elapsed" was adjusted to -0.25 to ensure that cash flows are being properly discounted. My 1.4 "Beta" is a modeling estimate that reflects CPRX’s risk profile. The company is predominantly dependent on just two products and has no internal R&D engine.

Most everything else is either standard DCF modeling methodology or was pulled from CPRX’s latest financial statements (e.g., 10-Q).

Based on my assumptions, CPRX is undervalued by ~26%.

Note that my "Yearly Projections" are roughly in line with the consensus revenue estimates:

When I perform a “reverse DCF” via my Sensitivity Analysis, it’s clear that the market is pricing CPRX for much lower growth.

It is also possible that while future cash flows (within the modeling period) are relatively assured, there may be great uncertainty surrounding CPRX’s “tail value,” or terminal value.

While the market’s view is relatively conservative, I wouldn’t flag it as ridiculous. It appears that the market is pricing the stock as if FIRDAPSE eventually plateaus, FYCOMPA erodes rapidly as more generics enter, and AGAMREE growth slows once the first big wave of DMD center conversions is done and payers tighten. Margins likely compress from ~40% due to higher R&D and/or BD spend.

Basically, CPRX may be a good investment if it supports mid-single-digit revenue growth for a decade, FCF margin in the mid-30s, IP holds, and management doesn’t destroy value with BD. This will likely be “easier said than done.”

Final Words

Catalyst definitely merits a rating upgrade to Hold. Its narrative has shifted from “looming LOE” (although still relevant to FYCOMPA) into a conservatively-priced, "cash-rich specialty pharma with two durable pillars” (FIRDAPSE and AGAMREE).

That said, this is a business dependent on just two products with bare minimum R&D. So, CPRX stock remains dependent on management’s ability to execute disciplined BD rather than discovery.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Gold Has A New Buyer, A Different Kind Of Central Bank
data.description:
In October, I wrote about the structural changes in the buyers of gold, and attributed much of the rise in the price of gold to ex-U.S. forces, namely central banks and the speculation of de-dollarizing economies (which is likely folly, but I don't dictate what other countries do).

Recent data has come out that changes my perspective on where the gold demand is coming from (it's still not the United States!), and it has made me more bullish than I was before. The structural changes are still underway.

Foreign Central Bank Demand Is Still Strong

Don't get me wrong here; I still believe that the major drivers of long-term gold demand are coming from outside the United States.

We know that it's not U.S. institutions (well, they are speculating, but not really allocating). We can see that in conservative institutions like family offices, allocation to gold is abysmally low.

I covered this in "Smart Money Is Reallocating." Here's my favorite chart from that article. It shows family office average allocation changes from the last two years.

Even though alternatives are on the rise, this hasn't included commodities. U.S. institutions are still eschewing direct gold exposure, despite its doubling in value over that time.

But foreign central banks, notably the People's Bank of China ("PBOC"), have been importing gold like nobody's business. PBOC's share of gold in its reserves is now the highest in modern history.

I detailed these purchases in October, but it's notable that since then, we've only seen further increases in purchases. Of course, this data is a little noisy, because it's based on IMF reporting, and some countries tend not to report fully (looking at you, PBOC). However, the trend remains intact for the year, and sales of gold have dropped off despite the paper profits many of these central banks are sitting on.

Following September, October set a new YTD record on purchases from central banks across the world. This is still below the November 2024 spike, which I believe was a response to the U.S. election.

But central banks are not speculators, and they are often more concerned with how much gold they have on hand than how much it's worth. This is less true for the new player on the scene, which had been buying gold before now but recently ratcheted up their efforts: cryptocurrency issuers.

Securing Stablecoins With Gold

Those in the cryptocurrency space will likely recognize the ticker USDT-USD, often just called "Tether." It's named after the company that issues it, and it is the world's largest stablecoin. These are cryptocurrencies chained to the value of a dollar. One USDT should always carry the same value as one dollar, and since we price the asset in dollars (or at least I do as an American), that should make the currency interchangeable with real dollars. Crypto skeptics will argue otherwise, but that is for another article.

When I first heard reports of Tether buying gold, this made a lot of sense to me. They also issue a cryptocurrency tied to the price of gold, XAUT-USD, and as they issue more of that coin, they need to buy the requisite amount of gold to back up the value.

But now, Tether has more gold on hand than the Shanghai Futures Exchange. This is a huge shift, and it happened quickly.

I knew that USDT was not entirely backed by dollars. Instead, it's backed by a mix of reserves, mostly dollars and deposits, but also Bitcoin and gold ("precious metals"). What's interesting is that this rush for gold wasn't just going to back XAUT issuance; most of it went to USDT's balance sheet.

From Q2 to Q3 this year, they increased their precious metals holdings on USDT's balance sheet from $8 billion to nearly $13 billion. That's a big jump in just one quarter.

Here are their current assets as of the last quarterly report:

When we compare this to central bank demand, we're starting to see Tether alone take up more and more of the total demand. Keep in mind, the "central banks" bar is all the world's central banks combined, including the PBOC.

What's most fascinating is when you see Tether compared to these central banks individually. It really shows how staggering this recent accumulation has been. This is just for Q3 this year.

It's Unlikely That Others Will Follow

Here's the tricky part. Immediately, I think there will be many people who jump to -- well, if Tether's doing this, then demand for gold from other stablecoin issuers like the second-largest USDC-USD, issued by Circle (CRCL) should follow.

There's a wrench in the plans. One named "GENIUS." Passed earlier this year, the Genius Act requires that U.S.-based stablecoin issuers have full 1:1 reserves backing the stablecoins they issue, and these reserves can only be held in deposits and in Treasuries.

Here is the relevant text from the bill:

(1) In general.—A permitted payment stablecoin issuer shall—

(A) maintain identifiable reserves backing the outstanding payment stablecoins of the permitted payment stablecoin issuer on an at least 1 to 1 basis, with reserves comprising—

(i) United States coins and currency (including Federal Reserve notes) or money standing to the credit of an account with a Federal Reserve Bank;

(ii) funds held as demand deposits (or other deposits that may be withdrawn upon request at any time) or insured shares at an insured depository institution (including any foreign branches or agents, including correspondent banks, of an insured depository institution), subject to limitations established by the Corporation and the National Credit Union Administration, as applicable, to address safety and soundness risks of such insured depository institution;

(iii) Treasury bills, notes, or bonds—

(I) with a remaining maturity of 93 days or less; or

(II) issued with a maturity of 93 days or less;

(iv) money received under repurchase agreements, with the permitted payment stablecoin issuer acting as a seller of securities and with an overnight maturity, that are backed by Treasury bills with a maturity of 93 days or less;

(V) reverse repurchase agreements, with the permitted payment stablecoin issuer acting as a purchaser of securities and with an overnight maturity, that are collateralized by Treasury notes, bills, or bonds on an overnight basis, subject to overcollateralization in line with standard market terms...

This means that U.S.-based issuers will be unable to hold any gold on their balance sheet. Could they issue gold-backed coins like XAUT-USD? Absolutely. But this demand may be isolated to Tether since they are the only large stablecoin issuer outside the United States.

That being said, Tether makes up 60% of the stablecoin market globally. So even though other issuers won't follow this change and the strategic shift from Tether is very bullish for gold, I don't see a world in which the purchases slow down any time soon given how stablecoins are becoming more and more popular.

BRICS Currency Speculation

All of this doesn't account for the fact that I believe in the next five years we will see a BRICS-centric payment system that relies on a basket of precious metals (of which gold will likely dominate).

For the record, BRICS is an informal economic alliance between Brazil, Russia, India, China, South Africa, and the economies aligned to them ("BRICS+").

Before I get any comments of you're crazy, nothing can ever replace the dollar, I doubt that a BRICS currency would actually be aimed as a global dollar replacement but as a means of dollar avoidance by the specific economies in question.

I don't even think it would be used in the countries that are part of BRICS. A BRICS currency would not be designed to replace the Renminbi, Rupee, Real, Ruble, Rand, etc. (first time I've realized that all the BRICS currencies start with R), from my view. Instead, it would be used as a medium between these countries and likely between oil producers in OPEC.

To get the Saudis and Emirates to de-dollarize, something that China has long wanted, they would need something that is as trusted as the dollar, since the BRICS currencies mentioned above are not as trustworthy as the dollar due to devaluation and authoritarian practices in the governments in regard to fiat currencies.

A currency backed by a basket of precious metals and redeemable for those precious metals at the central banks, like the PBOC, could make a lot of sense.

There just isn't enough gold for it to be entirely gold-backed. But if we added silver, platinum, and palladium to the mix, things start to check out a little more. Look at where these metals are mined. Of special note is the monopoly BRICS has on platinum and palladium mining.

Like I said, I don't think this is a replacement for domestic currencies, but rather it will be mostly digitally transferred as a means of having a hard currency that can pay for oil, which is currently almost completely dollarized.

U.S. sanctions on Lukoil and Rosneft only complicate this because they're forcing China and India away from Russian oil and into the dollarized trade coming out of the Middle East. Over time, I expect the dollar paradigm to be challenged, and that will be a significant catalyst for the prices of most precious metals, gold in particular.

This is not something that will happen immediately, but slowly and quietly. The indicator to me that it has begun is the rise in central bank purchases in the first place, especially the PBOC, which ramped up gold purchases right after we weaponized the dollar and froze $300 billion in Russian assets following their invasion of Ukraine.

How I'm Buying Gold Exposure

I am still very bullish on gold, mostly for the reasons I've outlined here and in my last report. The structural shifts in demand are not coming from the United States, and U.S. institutions still just look like speculators. This is changing quickly, and shifts like that of Tether are very indicative that not only are central banks hoarding gold, but now so are cryptocurrency issuers. That opens up a whole new lane for gold demand.

Currently, my largest gold-related holding has become GDMN, which I covered last month. It is a fund that owns both gold and global gold miners. This is what I recommend to most people because it allows you to take a smaller stance and get more exposure to both gold and the miners that dig it up, which often can rise in value faster than gold itself during these bullish cycles.

Conclusion

I see the structural demand for gold as growing, and this includes not only central banks but now also another kind of central bank: cryptocurrency issuers. Gold is being bought up as part of a means of backing USDT, which makes up 60% of the stablecoin market. This is a huge shift, and one that shouldn't be ignored.

Of course, speculation is still rampant, and gold's recent volatility has proven that while U.S. investors are not accumulating and allocating to gold like those in the rest of the world, they are still trading it heavily. I expect that volatility to continue moving forward, but that is not necessarily a bad thing. It does warrant caution.

As always, and especially with gold now, this line is prescient:

Position sizing is often more important than security selection.

Thanks for reading.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Alimentation Couche-Tard: Timeless Upside From Food, But Uncertain At $74/Share
data.description:
Note: Unless noted, dollar amounts are in CAD.

So, Alimentation Couche-Tard (ATD:CA), or simply Couche-Tard is a Canadian multinational food (among other things) retailer. For those of you who are familiar with my work, you know that I've, over the years, spent a lot of time and capital investing in and following up on food companies. Not only in Sweden or the Nordics, which is my main "home", but also in areas like Eastern Europe, Portugal, Brazil, the USA, to now entering Canada, and look at this company.

This company operates a lot of convenience stores and gas stations under several brands. The most noteworthy of the brands that the company owns is not, in my perspective, even Couche-tard, but Circle K. Circle K is one of the foremost fuel stations and chains in the entire world, and it's also the brand that the company uses most often outside of Canada.

Aside from Circle K, we're looking at brands like Holiday Superstores, like Couche-Tard, like Ingo (fuel, but unmanned), Irving Oil, and others. The company includes both franchises (like "On the Run") as well as its wholly owned brands.

ATD has been on a mission to simplify its brands, which is why most of the fuel stations on an international basis have been replaced with Circle K branding. In the Nordics, this was one of the things they did, when they agreed to buy the Norwegian Statoil Fuel and Retail for almost $3B. This gave the company market leadership in the Scandinavian and somewhat Baltic fuel market.

So, Alimentation Couche-Tard is a multinational leader in fuel (and lately also charging, because the company's fuel stations are obviously adding this as well), as well as groceries in key areas.

In this article, I will try to determine if this company is a good idea to invest in during a market that's seeing what I consider to be significant overvaluation.

What appeals to me in the company's fundamentals is that the company is clearly preparing to, and has already entered, what I consider to be growth markets in both ME, Asia, and Africa.

So let's see what we have here.

Alimentation Couche-Tard - Upside from operations across the world

It's fair to call the company more of a convenience chain than a grocery store, even if the company does sell grocery items. Much of its sales are done in conjunction however, with fuel and convenience, and that's where most of its operations are found.

The company generates annual revenues justifying a market capitalization of nearly $70B(CAD), with a rating of BBB+ in terms of credit. What drew me to ATD, aside from these operational specifics you see above, is the company's forecast accuracy. More than 30-55% of the time, depending on if you look at the 1-year or 2-year basis, ATD beats targets and estimates. Growth for this company is forecasted at over 13% per year over the next 3-4 years, and together with an admittedly low dividend of 1.16% at today's price, this makes the company interesting at the right price.

The company's market opportunities for this significant growth forecast lies in merging fragmented markets, similar to how it has done in Scandinavia and Sweden. With Circle K and Ingo, the only real competition offered the company is now 1-2 major chains. Smaller fuel and petrol stations here are rare. So market fragmentation here is low - but this is not the case in say, the US.

ATD is focusing on this fragmented market for further and future upside.

ATD also has a proven history of being able to grow both sales and EBIT significantly over time. Its method of doing so has been inorganic growth, meaning M&A's and additions. Over the past 15-20 years, sales for the company have grown for over 15 consecutive years.

The company has a nearly 50-year history, and its current assets encompass over 17,000 convenience stores across the world. Most of the company's sales, aside from straight fuel, are tobacco products and fresh grocery items. As mentioned, an important part of its expansion that it has been pushing here in Scandinavia is its renewable charging network, and it has opened some of its first EV-only charging hubs.

It's fair to say that the company is "in the time", and regardless of which way the EV automotive revolution goes, the company will not fail because it has failed to prepare for e-mobility. Current targets are over 1,000 ultra-fast charging points within the next 13 months.

In the US, competition for Couche-tard is primarily Murphy (MUSA) and Casey's (CASY). I will be looking at those investment alternatives in the valuation section.

The problems with Couche-tard are primarily related to its inorganic growth focus. The company has been part of several failed or bad deals and M&A's, and this also remains the single most destructive potential on a forward basis. The latest target for ATD was 7-Eleven, and thankfully, the company backed off from this deal, largely to avoid dilution. This was in July of this year. Carrefour (OTCPK:CRARY) was also stopped by the French government (which I am happy about, being a Carrefour shareholder and believing the company worth far more), as well as a failed Speedway Deal, though this was now 4 years ago.

But these risks are not to be overstated. The company is, after all, not just around any longer, but is also thriving, and has been able to successfully integrate large brands and chains into its operations. I would characterize the company as a skilled capital allocator, though one that needs to be viewed with a close eye for shareholder dilution and value destruction. Also, I view it as less of a risk these days, because as big as the company is now (and with the declines that we've seen), the remaining targets for constructive M&A's are relatively few. Together with successful strategies such as private brands, as seen above, or expanding food, I see potential for upside, which I obviously am not the only one to see, given the company's forecasted upside.

As for the latest results, they essentially confirm the company's upside. Strong earnings growth of 5.4%, growth in merchandise and service returns, and a significant increase in net earnings. Canada saw most of that growth, with European macro being reflective of poor/slow growth. If you consider the company to be interesting, the biggest things to watch out for would, in my view, be home-market (Canadian, US) margins. The big changes able to be driven by the company is a more efficient handling of its selection. it requires the further (and larger use) of private labels in the company's stores, the reduction of other labels and products. Much of the margin improvements come from that - and if the company manages to continue on this road, there's a meaningful margin improvement possible, even if at low sales increases. This also concerns distribution efficiency.

It's worth at least mentioning that US sales trends differ wildly across states. Some are up over 5%, but some major areas for company sales mix are negative in sales (like Texas and Arizona, together over 22% of merchandise sales). So it's worth mentioning that volatility should be accounted for.

With good results out of the way, let's look at the valuation for the company here.

Alimentation Couche-Tard valuation - almost impossible to get cheap

This isn't the first or even the fourth time I've looked at ATD. Over time, it has become clear to me that getting this company at an undervaluation is very tricky. It's not currently undervalued either, as I see it. A significant dip in earnings was recorded last year and in the last fiscal year, which is fairly unique for the business. But current forecasts are back to growth, with a 20-year average P/E of 18x.

Unfortunately, this is where we run into issues. Even at the high expectation for ATD, even with a 13%+ annualized EPS growth, and even allowing for that 18x P/E, the current upside for the company is less than 13% per year. Part of the culprit is the dividend, but the fact remains that this company is not currently cheap.

Even with a high upside like that, you're not getting what I generally would look for. When looking at alternatives, as in alternative investments, there's Casey's, which shares some of ATD's appeal in terms of forecast beating, but is also significantly overvalued at almost twice that of ATD. The company also has a marginal yield of less than 0.5% per share, which makes it even less attractive at this price. The other alternative is Murphy US, and this is probably the cheapest of the lot. However, MUSA also comes with problems. Less growth at below 9% per year, a BB+ junk-rated credit as opposed to ATD's BBB+, and a market capitalization that is less than a third of that of ATD. With that comparison, even the lower valuation and potential upside are not enough to make me interested in an investment there.

I would characterize the sector as one to keep a very close eye on in the event of a downturn, but would not invest in ATD or any of its peers at this time.

In terms of introductory price, I give ATD a fair value of 18.8x forward P/E, of about $97/share, which means that my PT for the company comes to about $68/share for a clear, 15% annualized upside even in the case of less positive returns than expected.

Risks & Considerations

The primary risks to this company with my current rating is the failure of inorganic growth as well as the macro. This is one of those companies where other risks, as I see them, are few and far between. Because of its sheer size, the cash generation it has, ATD is able to weather most any storm or downturn. With its expansion into charging, the e-mobility argument against the company does not work either. It's already in a good position to be able to manage this.

There is the possibility that in a shift in mobility, declining fuel sales could hurt ATD appeal and returns as they normalize and find their new levels, with a slight drop off with people being able to "fuel" their cars at home through electric. But I believe there will be an organic evolution here, and that the shift will not be as excessive as some seem to believe.

Based on this, ATD has the following thesis for me.

Thesis

While this company represents a very solid potential holding in a conservative portfolio, combining all the best parts of fuel and convenience store sales, the upside is only good enough if you buy it at a cheap enough price, and if you're forecasting it at a reasonable forward P/E.

At the current valuation, my preference is to "wait". The company's 18.8x P/E is too high for the average, and for the upside, even with double-digit growth. It would require over 19-20x P/E for a 15% annualized rate of return, and I don't see that in the company's cards here.

For that reason, and not due to fundamental appeal, I consider ATD to be a "HOLD" here, and would wait for a share price of below $68/share.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $68/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of December 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dollar Tree's Halloween Sales Were Scary Good, But There's A Twist
data.description:
Dollar Tree's Q3 2025 Earnings Review

Retailer earnings week has started, and Dollar Tree (DLTR) is up 4% on the day of its earnings release.

Last quarter, we saw the company dismissing its Family Dollar business for around $1B to focus on its core discount business. Now, we find it reporting strong momentum and a record-breaking Halloween season. Sales reached $4.7B, up 9.4% YoY, with same-store sales up by 4.2% (avg. ticket up 4.5%, traffic down 0.3%). Even with the impact of tariffs, DLTR was able to increase its gross margin by 40 bps to 35.8%. The report was a beat both on the top and the bottom line, with EPS of $1.21 coming in $0.13 above the consensus.

If we zoom out and look at the first nine months of the year, things are going well. Net sales are up 11% to $13.9B, with same store sales up 5.4% (1.8% traffic; 3.6% average ticket).

Moreover, the company disclosed its Q4 outlook, expecting comparable sales to grow between 4% and 6%. This means FY sales should come just shy of $19.5B while FY EPS should be around $5.70, reflecting the favorable impact of share repurchases (YtD: $1.31B for 15M shares; with the addition of another 1.7M shares bought after the end of Q3 for $176M).

When we look at the trends that Dollar Tree discloses regarding how its comparable sales have developed since 2022, we see that Q3 was the first quarter since Q4 2022 with negative traffic. At the same time, Dollar Tree is now on a 5-quarter streak of higher average ticket, where we see favorable momentum as we get ready to close 2025 and head into 2026.

This is confirmed by a number Dollar Tree disclosed during the earnings call: 3M more households shopped at its stores in Q3 2025 versus Q3 2024. Interestingly, we heard that:

Approximately 60% of these incremental shoppers came from higher-income households, those earning over $100,000, 30% from middle-income households, those earning between $60,000 to $100,000 with the rest from lower-income households, those earning under $60,000. Importantly, Q3 spending growth was broad-based across all income subcohorts, including households earning below $20,000.

Surely, a big part of Dollar Tree's current success comes from its pivot to a multi-price strategy that the company adopted back in 2019, and that is now attracting more and more customers. Dollar Tree management took as an example its Halloween sales and compared its 2022 mix versus this year's:

To see the full impact of multi-price, let's go back to Halloween 2022, when multi-price was still in its infancy. That year, multi-price represented about 3% of units sold, 10% of sales, and 7% of merchandise gross margin across our full Halloween assortment. Fast forward to 2025 on a 25% larger base of sales, where multi-price accounted for roughly 1/4 of our total Halloween sales and merchandise gross margin, but only 8% of Halloween units sold.

Moreover, we learned that every multi-price item generates 3.5x more profits compared to the non-multi-price items that the company sells. In hindsight, we must acknowledge that Dollar Tree's strategy is paying off.

Below, we see that both DLTR and its main competitor, Dollar General (DG), have been experiencing sustained foot traffic. We have discussed this every time we have dealt with retailers this year: we are seeing lower-income consumers becoming cost-conscious and adopting trade-down behaviors, seeking value, and reducing the volume of their purchases. In this environment, we have heard people talking about inflation fatigue and an economy at two speeds. Probably, had it not been for the outbreak of AI, the economy might have experienced a recession, but currently, the U.S. GDP is pushed up by the largest investment cycle the world has ever seen.

Dollar Tree's Valuation

There is no doubt that the company is seeing favorable momentum and that its execution has enabled it to grow at a faster pace compared to many of its peers. The current environment, with inflation still pressuring many consumers, is perfect for the company to thrive and expand. But Dollar Tree's success doesn't rely only on favorable tailwinds; it also comes from some choices that its management took to focus more and more on the core value proposition.

So, is Dollar Tree a buy right now? Well, it currently trades at a fwd PE of 19.2, which is below the market average. Its fwd EV/EBITDA is 12.7, which is a bit expensive as the sector median is 10.4. However, this is not an exuberant multiple, and it is more or less in line with Dollar General's multiples. Dollar Tree has outperformed its main peer this year, and this makes sense since its execution has been impeccable. Today, DLTR is not, therefore, a screaming bargain, trading more or less around fair value. I would not give Dollar Tree an earnings multiple in the low to mid-20s. After all, there are some risks, as consumer spending sensitivity is something this business always has to face. In this case, the risk is not a recession, but a rebound. If consumers feel less inflationary pressure, higher-income households could go back to their usual retailers. All in all, while DLTR keeps generating stable EBITDA and cash flows, the fair EV/EBITDA multiple stays between 11 and 13. So, I would not be aggressive in buying the stock right now. If you already own it, there is no reason to sell, as you are probably sitting on some nice gains. But for people who have not bought the stock in previous years, this is not the right moment to buy it. Therefore, I stick to my neutral rating.

data.title: IJH: Core Mid-Cap Equities As A Foundation For A Diversified Portfolio Strategy
data.description:
The iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH) is a passively managed, low-cost exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with broad exposure to US mid-cap equities. The strategy is widely adopted as a core equity strategy with over $100b in net assets and provides substantial trading depth for active portfolio rotation. IJH can be utilized by investors as the foundation for building a portfolio, actively rotating out of IJH into satellite positions as new investment opportunities arise.

About iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF

IJH was launched by iShares on May 22, 2000 on the NYSE Arca Exchange. IJH has a low expense ratio of 5bps and pays out a modest distribution rate of $0.83/share with a 30-day SEC yield of 1.21%. IJH is benchmarked to the S&P Mid-Cap 400 Index as provided by S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC.

The Index largely covers US mid-cap equities with a market capitalization of $7.4-$20.5b at the time of inclusion. The Index weighs constituents based on market capitalization, meaning that larger mid-cap companies will have a bigger influence on the Index when compared to smaller companies. For example, the top 10 holdings account for 7.65% of the total portfolio weight whereas the bottom 10 holdings make up 0.43% of net assets. Though the top holdings make up a larger weight, the level of concentration risk is relatively minimal, particularly when compared to large-cap indices like the S&P 500 (SPX) where the top 10 constituents account for 39.51% of the portfolio weight. Given that the top holding in IJH, Comfort Systems USA (FIX), has a portfolio weight of roughly 1.07%, I do not believe any single holding will have substantial influence over the performance of the portfolio.

IJH is heavily weighted in industrials, making up 23.72% of the portfolio weight. Other large sector weightings include financials at 16.38%, information technology at 13.69%, and consumer discretionary at 11.65%. By comparison, the S&P 500 has a sector concentration of 35.29% in technology, 12.79% in financials, and 10.98% in communications. Given the sector weights, I believe IJH provides substantially more sector diversification while limiting exposure to the top constituents.

Looking at peer mid-cap strategies, investors may consider the State Street SPDR Portfolio S&P 400 Mid Cap ETF (SPMD) or the Vanguard Mid Cap Index Fund ETF (VO) as alternatives to IJH, each offering a slightly lower expense ratio with a relatively similar strategy.

VO will be a more concentrated investment strategy with 293 individual holdings, far below IJH and SPMD. Given that we’re considering IJH as a core position, I believe diversification will be a critical factor for fund selection as equity or sector concentration can be added through additional investment strategies.

Investor Suitability

IJH was designed to be used as a core mid-cap strategy, meaning that IJH can be used as a foundational position in a portfolio with satellite positions added to adjust equity or sector concentration. Unlike the S&P 500, IJH will provide investors with a larger concentration in the industrials sector which may be appealing for investors seeking non-AI-related investments.

Though IJH pays out a quarterly distribution rate annualized at $0.83/share, I do not believe the 1.26% yield necessarily makes the portfolio strategy appealing from an income perspective.

I believe the best way to utilize IJH is to position one’s equity strategy around IJH. From a tactical perspective, investors may utilize IJH as a placeholder for unallocated funds in their equity portfolio, rotating funds from IJH to other ETFs or equities to gain exposure to other markets that may be underrepresented in IJH. Taking into consideration sector weightings, investors may consider adding energy or communications exposure by adding an ETF or equity investments.

IJH can also be used for macroeconomic traders that may utilize a more thematic approach to making investment decisions. For example, mid-cap equities may outperform large-caps during an interest rate reduction cycle as these companies, particularly those in the industrial sector, may be more sensitive to the cost of financing debt.

IJH can be used for both long-term and short-term investment horizons. IJH has roughly $100b in net assets with an average of $657mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis, providing investors with substantial liquidity and trading depth for actively managing a position.

IJH is a passively managed strategy designed to provide investors with exposure to the S&P Mid-Cap 400 Index. Given that this is a passively managed strategy, investors will be responsible for tactical positioning as the fund will not adjust the strategy based on market conditions. Sector concentration may limit growth potential for IJH given the smaller allocation to the technology sector.

Final Thoughts

IJH can be an appealing core mid-cap equity holding for investors seeking to actively manage a portfolio strategy. IJH can best be utilized as a foundation or a placeholder for capital as an investor builds a portfolio of satellite positions around the core equity holding.

This article answers these three main questions about IJH:

  • Does IJH offer diversification away from large-cap tech names that dominate the broader indices?
  • Is IJH actively managed or passively managed?
  • What type of investor is IJH most suitable for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: TIC Solutions: Upside Is Visible, But The Company Needs To Prove Itself
data.description:
TIC Solutions (TIC) is a service company dedicated to testing, inspection and certification services and engineering. In its current form, TIC is a combination of two companies, Acuren and NV5 Global, and their integration has just begun. TIC Solutions is focused on energy, infrastructure and industrial end markets.

TIC Solutions reminds me of an investment case on Atlas Technical Consultants, which I published in 2022. Atlas Technical Consultants was taken public as a SPAC and it had a little messy financials, high debt and a series of acquisitions behind it. Like TIC Solutions, Atlas was engaged in testing, inspection and certification and technical design. In 2023, the company was acquired by a private equity firm which paid 124% premium and EV/EBITDA multiple of 11.5x.

Also Acuren was listed through a SPAC in 2025. Similarly to Atlas, many of its services are mandatory to its customers due to regulation and compliance, therefore a large part of its revenue is sticky and recurring. As a service provider, its business does not tie up capital and delivers strong cash flow. Both companies have a strong track record of acquisitive growth and resilience over cycles. The early results of the integration of the two companies are promising and the shares have upside if the company can meet the higher end of its guidance.

More diversified combination of two equals

TIC Solutions has three main segments, Inspection & Mitigation, Consulting Engineering and Geospatial. Most of its $2 billion revenue comes from the U.S., primarily from customers in infrastructure, energy and industrial sectors. One fifth of the business originates from the public-sector. TIC Solutions has 11 000 employees and over 250 locations. EY Parthenon estimates the global testing, inspection and certification market to grow 4-5% annually.

In 2024, NV5 posted a revenue of approximately $1 billion, so the merger is a combination of almost equal sized companies. Acuren’s main service lines included non-destructive testing, rope access and engineering and laboratory testing, whereas NV5’s focus was on consulting engineering and geospatial services. Now, the new entity is more diversified in terms of end markets and services although NV5’s margins were lower than Acuren’s.

In the first nine months, TIC Solutions grew revenue by 5%. The third quarter came in slower, proforma revenue growth of 2.4%. In Q3, adjusted EBITDA margin declined by 0.6 %-points to 16.3% but was higher than YTD margin of 15.4%. On top of the merger, TIC Solutions has made four bolt-on acquisitions this year, which support future growth.

The company is in a show-me situation

In the merger the owners of NV5 received cash and shares worth $23 per share, a 32% premium. Including the debt, the deal resulted in an enterprise value of $1.7 billion. The price paid equaled EV/EBITDA-multiple of approximately 12x. In 2024, NV5 grew revenue by 10% mainly thanks to acquisitions and adjusted EBITDA grew by 7%. It had a modest leverage ratio of 1.4x.

NV5 brought in Consulting Engineering and Geospatial revenue. However before the deal it pursued to highlight its testing, inspection and certification offering. Although NV5 did not disclose its revenue split by activity, this is most likely one of the main motivations for the merger. Now, NV5 revenue is grouped into two slices, Consulting Engineering and Geospatial, of the pie chart above describing the revenue split between the segments.

NV5’s businesses take the combined company closer to a couple of growth opportunities. Naturally, datacenters are one of them. Its sales to the datacenter sector “more than doubled” over the last 12 months. The sales to datacenters is only 3% of total revenue, but so far it is delivering only a limited amount of services to the sector, according to the Q3 earnings call. Expanding the offering should provide a long growth runway in the vertical.

TIC has highlighted the opportunities for cross-selling as a justification for the merger as TIC pursues to sell more services to the clients of each company. It has presented compelling case stories, however investors should remain cautious. Customers could already have their trusted partners in both lines of businesses and changing the service provider might be more difficult and take longer than initially expected.

TIC Solutions has launched some new services created by a combination of assets and expertise from the two merged companies. One such opportunity is expanding NV5’s services to Canada, where Acuren generated 43% of its revenue. Surprisingly fast TIC has come up and implemented entirely new offerings that combine NV5’s capabilities and Acuren’s field force.
